title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Motivating example
Context:

Interested in high-value protein production process
Subject matter experts think the current operating conditions may be sub-optimal
Subject matter experts have identified seven (\(7\)) factors may affect the process yield (\(Y\)),

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Motivating example
Goal:

Screen relevant factors that affect the process
Find the optimal yield

Constraint: Minimize experimental runs, because laboratory experiments are:

Expensive
Time-consuming
Resource-consuming

there is so much parameter space to explore!

To facilitate the explanation, we focus on factors A and B.


But the ideas presented here apply equally to the remaining factors.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield

Step 1: Identify relevant factors
Subject matter experts determine the restricted region of interest within the seven factor space, illustrated using factors A and B.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield

Step 1: Identify relevant factors
Subject matter experts determine the restricted region of interest within the seven factor space, illustrated using factors A and B.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Step 1: Identify relevant factors
Two options:

A \(2^{k}\) full factorial design (CRFD) [1]:

Benefit: estimates the intercept, all \(7\) main effects, \(21\) two-factor interactions, \(7\) pure quadratic terms, and higher-order effects.
Drawback: requires \(2^{k} = 2^{7} = 128\) experimental runs.

A resolution III fractional factorial design (CRFF) [1]:

Benefit: requires only \(2^{k-p} = 2^{7-4} = 8\) experimental runs when using \(p=4\) generators.
Drawbacks: estimates only the intercept and the \(7\) main effects, has no \(\text{df}\), and main effects are aliased with two-factor interactions.


Because we are screening we choose the second option.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Step 1: Identify relevant factors
The chosen resolution III CRFF design have the following characteristics:




[1] "Aliases"


                    
 A = B:D = C:E = F:G
 B = A:D = C:F = E:G
 C = A:E = B:F = D:G
 D = A:B = C:G = E:F
 E = A:C = B:G = D:F
 F = A:G = B:C = D:E
 G = A:F = B:E = C:D


Three principles

Effect Sparsity-Pareto S(P):
around 80% of the variability is explained by 20% of the factors
Hierarchical ordering H:
main effects are more likely to be important than two-factor interactions, and this more important than three-factor interactions and so on.
Heredity-Marginality H(M):
usually interactions occur between factors where at least one of the two main effects are significant.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Step 2: Steepest ascend
We model the local yield surface using response surface methodology (RSM) [1].



Call:

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Step 3: Bayesian Optimization
Within this high-yield region, we implement Bayesian Optimization (BO) [2, 3].
Specifically:

We use relevant initial training data:
Three points from the steepest ascend procedure located within the high-yield region.
We use a surrogate model to approximate the yield surface for a set of candidate data points:
A Gaussian Process with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel [3, 4].
We use an acquisition function to determine the next point to evaluate in the search space:
The Lower Confidence Bound (LCB) function.
We define a stopping criterion to determine when to terminate the search:
A central composite design [1] requires a total of \(12\) runs: \(4\) factorial points, \(4\) axial (face) points, and \(4\) center points.
Our BO procedure will use only \(8\) runs.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
The reality
The “unknown” true model for protein yield (\(Y\)) was:
\[
Y = 85 - 0.4(A-12)^2 - 0.02(B-70)^2 + \sum_{j=3}^{7} 0.01 \cdot X_{j} + 0.1(A-12)(B-70) + e; \quad \; e \sim N(0, 2)
\]
“unknown” true protein yield surface:



In reality the true model is not available to us.
However, simulation is useful to showcase the potential of the experimental strategies.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
FAIR principles in practice
This presentation is built to be a research object, not just a slide deck.

Findable (F): Searchable text and structured metadata ensure the content is discoverable.
Next step: Provision of a DOI via Zenodo or OSF.
Accesible (A): Publicly hosted on GitHub.
Anyone with an internet connection can retrieve the slides and underlying data.
Interoperable (I): Built using Open Source (Quarto/R).
Seamlessly integrates into any analysis workflow and automated pipelines.
Reusable (R): BSD 3 Licensed.
Includes all R dependencies and source code to fully replicate every simulation and analysis.

text: Questions?

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Annex: additional considerations
In a real-world setting, the identification of those \(7\) factors probably comes from experiments that seek to determine Sources of Variability. e.g.,

RSE (Random Statistical Error): Natural fluctuations in biological systems.
NSE (Non-Systematic Error): Potential issues with equipment or batch effects.

title: Optimizing Protein Yield
text:
Annex: additional considerations
What happens when the problem grows?

Multiple Objectives: If we need high Yield but low Waste, we employ Desirability Functions to find the “Sweet Spot” that balances both.
Extrapolation:

Empirical models (RSM/BO) are dangerous outside their tested range.
If we must predict outside our data, we shift toward Theoretical/Mechanistic Models based on biological first principles.
